philosophy: Physics is not abstract equations on a chalkboard — it is the operating system of reality. Every time you walk, see color, hear music, feel warmth, or charge your phone, you are experiencing physics. This curriculum makes physics PERSONAL: every law, every force, every wave is connected to something the student already feels, sees, or does. The philosophy: understand the universe by understanding your daily experience of it.

1_motion_and_forces.philosophy: You have been doing physics since you took your first step. Walking, throwing, driving, falling — every movement you've ever made obeys Newton's laws. This topic doesn't introduce physics; it names what your body already knows.

must_cover: position is where you are relative to reference
displacement is change in position with direction
displacement can be zero when distance isn't
daily_life_hooks: Running a lap around a track — 400m distance, 0m displacement
GPS navigation: displacement is 'as the crow flies', distance is the route

must_cover: velocity is how fast AND which direction (vector)
average speed = total distance / total time
daily_life_hooks: Your car's speedometer shows speed, not velocity
A roller coaster at the top of a loop has the same speed going up and down but opposite velocity

must_cover: acceleration is rate of velocity change
can speed up slow down or change direction
negative acceleration doesn't always mean slowing
daily_life_hooks: The feeling of being pushed into your seat when a car accelerates
Slamming brakes: negative acceleration you FEEL in your body
Turning a corner at constant speed IS acceleration (direction changes)

must_cover: flat line on v-t = constant velocity
daily_life_hooks: Your phone's GPS tracking IS a position-time graph
Fitness trackers graph your speed over time — reading these graphs IS physics
How far does your car travel during braking? Kinematic equations answer this
How high does a ball go? How long before it hits the ground?
You exert a force every time you open a door, type on a keyboard, or stand up
Gravity is a force you've never stopped experiencing since birth

search_queries: objects at rest stay at rest
must_cover: object at rest stays at rest unless acted on
object in motion stays in motion unless acted on
daily_life_hooks: Seatbelts exist because of the first law — your body wants to keep moving when the car stops
A tablecloth trick works because dishes have inertia
Why you lean forward when a bus brakes suddenly

search_queries: Newton's second law F equals ma
daily_life_hooks: Pushing a shopping cart vs pushing a loaded truck — same force, very different acceleration
Why sports cars are light — less mass = more acceleration for the same engine force
Why it's harder to stop a freight train than a bicycle

must_cover: every action has equal opposite reaction
daily_life_hooks: Walking: you push Earth backward, Earth pushes you forward
Rockets work by pushing exhaust down; exhaust pushes rocket up — even in vacuum
When you punch a wall, the wall punches your fist back equally hard (that's why it hurts)

must_cover: depends on surfaces and normal force
daily_life_hooks: Without friction you can't walk, drive, or hold anything
Ice is dangerous because low friction — your foot pushes but ground doesn't push back enough
Brake pads NEED friction. Tires NEED friction. Your shoes NEED friction.
Every engineer who designs a bridge, building, or car starts with free body diagrams
Understanding forces on YOUR body: gravity pulling down, chair pushing up, friction keeping you still

must_cover: all objects fall at g = 9.8 m/s²
air resistance is why feather falls slower
daily_life_hooks: Apollo 15 hammer-feather drop on the Moon — both hit at same time
Skydiving: you accelerate until air resistance balances gravity (terminal velocity)
A dropped phone accelerates at 9.8 m/s² — every 0.1 second it's going 1 m/s faster
Every ball you've ever thrown follows a parabola
Basketball shooting: angle and speed determine the arc — physics of the perfect shot
Water from a garden hose traces a projectile path

must_cover: every mass attracts every other mass
daily_life_hooks: The Moon orbits Earth because of the same force that makes your apple fall
Astronauts aren't 'weightless' — they're in freefall (gravity is still pulling them)
Tides exist because the Moon's gravity pulls on Earth's oceans

2_energy_and_work.philosophy: Energy is the universe's currency. Everything that happens — from your muscles contracting to stars shining — is an energy transaction. This topic reveals that energy is never created or destroyed; it just changes form. Your body is an energy conversion machine.

daily_life_hooks: Carrying a heavy box across a room — gravity does zero work (perpendicular)
Pushing a car that won't move — you're exhausted but did zero physics work
A 60W light bulb converts 60 joules of electrical energy per second
Your body at rest uses about 80W — same as a bright light bulb

must_cover: depends on mass AND velocity squared
daily_life_hooks: Why car crashes at 60 mph are 4x more dangerous than at 30 mph — KE quadruples
A bullet is small but has enormous KE because velocity is squared
A roller coaster at the top of the hill: maximum PE, minimum KE
Hydroelectric dams convert water's PE into electricity — height = stored energy
Bungee jumping: your KE converts to elastic PE in the cord
Every time you walk, tendons store and release elastic PE

must_cover: energy cannot be created or destroyed
total energy constant in closed system
daily_life_hooks: When you 'use' energy, you're converting it — electrical to light, chemical to kinetic
The energy in your lunch came from sunlight → plants → food → your muscles → motion
Your phone: chemical (battery) → electrical → light + sound + heat
Pendulum: PE → KE → PE endlessly (minus friction)

3_momentum_and_collisions.philosophy: Why do airbags save lives? Why do martial artists break boards? Why does a pool break shot scatter balls predictably? Momentum is the physics of impact — and understanding it literally saves lives.

must_cover: more mass or speed = more momentum
daily_life_hooks: A truck at 30 mph has more momentum than a bicycle at 30 mph — that's why truck crashes are worse
A bullet has less mass than a bowling ball but can have more momentum

must_cover: same impulse with less force over more time
same impulse with more force over less time
daily_life_hooks: Airbags increase impact TIME → reduce impact FORCE → save lives
Bending knees when landing: same impulse, more time, less force on joints
Baseball: following through increases contact time with ball → more impulse → farther hit

must_cover: total momentum before = after in closed system
daily_life_hooks: Rocket propulsion: gas goes one way, rocket goes the other — total momentum stays zero
Ice skating: push off a wall and you slide — wall gains negligible momentum, you gain a lot
Car crashes: inelastic — crumple zones ABSORB KE as deformation (saving you)
Billiard balls: nearly elastic — hear the 'click' of KE transfer
Catching a ball: perfectly inelastic — ball and glove move together

4_circular_motion_and_rotation.philosophy: From spinning tops to orbiting planets, from car wheels to washing machines — circular motion is everywhere. This topic reveals why you feel pushed outward on a merry-go-round (you're not — it's inertia) and how satellites stay in orbit (they're constantly falling).

must_cover: speed can be constant while accelerating
daily_life_hooks: Turning a car: you feel pushed outward but it's your inertia — the CAR is being pushed inward
Spin cycle in washing machine: water goes straight (inertia) while drum curves inward

must_cover: not a new force — it's the NET inward force
can be gravity, tension, friction, normal
removes the force = object goes straight
daily_life_hooks: Moon orbiting Earth: gravity IS the centripetal force
Car turning: friction between tires and road IS the centripetal force
A yo-yo swung in a circle: string tension IS the centripetal force
Opening a door: push at the handle (far from hinge) = easy. Push near the hinge = hard. Same force, different torque.
Longer wrench = more torque = easier to loosen bolts
Why doorknobs are on the edge of doors, not the middle
Seesaws: heavier person sits closer to center to balance
Carrying a heavy bag: your body leans to shift center of mass over feet
Why tall buildings need wide bases

5_waves_and_sound.philosophy: Every sound you've ever heard — every song, every conversation, every thunder crack — is a pressure wave vibrating air molecules that hit your eardrum. Music IS physics. Speech IS physics. Your entire auditory experience of being alive is waves.

must_cover: transfer of energy without matter transfer
daily_life_hooks: Ocean waves: water moves up and down, the ENERGY moves forward — surfers ride energy, not water
The wave at a stadium: people stand and sit, but nobody moves sideways — the pattern moves
Radio stations: 101.5 FM = 101.5 million Hz — that's the wave's frequency
Volume = amplitude. Pitch = frequency. Bass = low frequency. Treble = high frequency
Echo = sound reflection
Straw looks bent in water = light refraction
Hearing someone around a corner = sound diffraction
Noise-canceling headphones = destructive interference
In space, no one can hear you scream — no air, no sound
Thunder arrives after lightning because sound is slower than light
Doctors use stethoscopes because sound travels well through solids
A guitar string tightened vibrates faster = higher pitch
Whisper ~30 dB, conversation ~60 dB, concert ~110 dB, jet engine ~140 dB (pain)
Dogs hear up to 65,000 Hz — dog whistles are above your hearing range but not theirs
Pushing a swing at the right moment = resonance — small pushes build to big swings
Tacoma Narrows Bridge collapse: wind frequency matched bridge's natural frequency
Musical instruments produce multiple harmonics — that's what gives each instrument its unique 'color'
Wine glass shattering from a singer's voice = resonance at the glass's natural frequency
Ambulance siren: higher pitch approaching, lower when leaving — the sound wave is being compressed/stretched
Police radar guns use Doppler effect with radio waves to measure car speed
Astronomers use Doppler shift of light to measure how fast galaxies move — redshift = moving away

6_light_and_optics.philosophy: Light is how you experience the universe. Every color you see, every image in a mirror, every photo on your phone — it's all light physics. Your eyes are optical instruments. Cameras are optical instruments. Rainbows are optical phenomena. Understanding light is understanding how you SEE.

must_cover: spectrum: radio → microwave → IR → visible → UV → X-ray → gamma
daily_life_hooks: WiFi is electromagnetic waves. So is the warmth from a fire (infrared). So is sunburn (UV). So is the X-ray of your broken bone. All the same phenomenon — different frequencies.
Your microwave oven uses electromagnetic waves tuned to water molecules' resonant frequency — that's why it heats food but not the plate

must_cover: objects appear colored by reflecting/absorbing wavelengths
daily_life_hooks: A red apple absorbs ALL colors except red — it reflects red to your eyes
A sunset is red because blue light scatters away — only red wavelengths reach you straight on
Your phone screen makes every color from just 3: red, green, blue pixels

must_cover: light behaves as wave AND particle
daily_life_hooks: Solar panels work because of particle nature — photons knock electrons loose
Digital cameras: each photon that hits a pixel deposits energy (photoelectric effect)
The double-slit experiment: single photons create interference pattern — one of physics' deepest mysteries

must_cover: angle of incidence = angle of reflection
daily_life_hooks: You see yourself in a lake because water is smooth = specular reflection
You can't see yourself in a wall because rough surface = diffuse reflection (light scatters)
Bike reflectors use retroreflectors — light bounces back the way it came, no matter the angle

must_cover: flat mirror: virtual, same size, reversed left-right
daily_life_hooks: 'Objects in mirror are closer than they appear' — convex mirrors demagnify but show wider view
Makeup mirrors are concave — magnify your face
Satellite dishes are concave to focus radio waves to a point
Spoon: concave side = inverted face, convex side = right-side-up face
Pool looks shallower than it is — refraction bends light from the bottom
Diamonds sparkle because high index of refraction = lots of internal reflection
Fiber optic cables use total internal reflection — light bounces inside and never escapes = internet
Your EYE has a converging lens that focuses images onto your retina
Nearsighted: eyeball too long, image focuses before retina → diverging lens corrects
Farsighted: eyeball too short → converging lens corrects
Camera lenses, microscopes, telescopes — all combinations of converging and diverging lenses
Pink Floyd's Dark Side of the Moon album cover IS a physics diagram
Every rainbow you've ever seen is light refracting and reflecting inside millions of water droplets
You never see the same rainbow as someone else — each person sees light from different droplets

7_electricity.philosophy: You live inside an electrical civilization. Every light, every screen, every appliance, every device in your life runs on moving electrons. Your brain runs on electricity. Your heart beats because of electrical signals. Understanding electricity is understanding the power that runs your world AND your body.

daily_life_hooks: Static shock on a doorknob: electrons jumped from you to the metal
Laundry static cling: rubbing in dryer transfers electrons between fabrics
Lightning: charge separation in clouds → massive discharge
The electric force between an electron and proton in hydrogen is 10³⁹ times stronger than their gravitational attraction
All of chemistry — bonding, reactions, materials — is electric force between charged particles

must_cover: water analogy: voltage=pressure, current=flow, resistance=pipe width
daily_life_hooks: Your wall outlet: 120V (US) — that's the 'push'. The device determines how much current flows.
A phone charger: converts 120V to ~5V because your phone's circuits need less 'push'
Your nervous system: neurons fire at about 100 millivolts — 1,200 times less than a wall outlet
Dimmer switches work by adding resistance → less current → dimmer light
Wet skin has much lower resistance than dry skin — that's why electricity in water is so dangerous
A toaster's heating element has specific resistance designed to convert electrical energy to heat

must_cover: series: one path, same current, voltages add
parallel: multiple paths, same voltage, currents add
parallel: one break doesn't affect others
daily_life_hooks: Old Christmas lights were series — one bulb burns out, whole string goes dark
Your house wiring is parallel — one light off doesn't kill them all
Why your phone has a circuit breaker — parallel circuits can draw too much total current

must_cover: electrical bill measures energy not power
daily_life_hooks: Your electric bill is in kilowatt-hours: 1 kWh = using 1000W for 1 hour
A 100W bulb for 10 hours = 1 kWh ≈ 12 cents
Why power lines use high voltage: same power delivered with less current = less heat loss

must_cover: magnets have north and south poles
field lines: north to south outside
daily_life_hooks: Compass points north because Earth IS a giant magnet
MRI machines use incredibly powerful magnets to image your body
Fridge magnets: tiny magnetic domains aligned in the material

must_cover: can turn on/off unlike permanent magnets
daily_life_hooks: Every electric motor: electromagnetism converts electrical energy to rotation
MRI, speakers, electric locks, junkyard cranes — all electromagnets
Your doorbell is an electromagnet that pulls a clapper against a bell
Every power plant: turbines spin magnets near coils → electricity
Wireless phone charging: changing magnetic field induces current in your phone
Electric guitar pickups: vibrating steel string changes magnetic field → induces current → amplified as sound
Generators are motors running backwards

8_thermodynamics.philosophy: Why does coffee cool down? Why can't you unscramble an egg? Why does sweating cool you? Thermodynamics is the physics of heat, energy flow, and the arrow of time. It explains why some things happen spontaneously and others never do — the deepest insight into the nature of reality.

must_cover: heat: energy transfer due to temp difference
daily_life_hooks: Metal feels colder than wood at the same temperature because metal CONDUCTS heat away from your hand faster
You don't feel temperature — you feel heat TRANSFER. That's why 70°F air feels fine but 70°F water feels cold (water conducts heat away 25x faster)
Cooking: pan conducts heat, boiling water convects, broiler radiates
Your body: conduction (touching cold metal), convection (wind chill), radiation (feeling a fire's warmth from across the room)
Thermos bottle blocks all three: vacuum (no conduction/convection), silver lining (reflects radiation)
Beach sand scorches your feet but ocean water is cool — sand heats fast (low specific heat), water resists temperature change (high specific heat)
Coastal cities have milder climates because nearby ocean absorbs/releases huge amounts of heat slowly
Car engines use water coolant specifically because water absorbs enormous amounts of heat before getting hot

must_cover: internal energy changes by heat added minus work done
daily_life_hooks: Your body: food energy (Q in) = body heat + movement (W out) + stored energy (ΔU)
Air pump gets hot: work done ON air converts to internal energy → temperature rises

must_cover: heat flows spontaneously hot → cold only
daily_life_hooks: You can't unscramble an egg, unmix cream from coffee, or unbreak a glass — entropy explains WHY
Your body maintains low entropy by constantly consuming energy (food) and exporting entropy (heat, waste) — you're an entropy-exporting machine
The arrow of time: the reason the past looks different from the future is the second law

9_modern_physics.philosophy: The 20th century revealed that at the smallest scales and highest speeds, the universe plays by different rules than Newton imagined. Atoms are mostly empty space. Time slows down at high speeds. Particles can be in two places at once. Modern physics isn't science fiction — it's the foundation of every piece of technology you use: GPS, smartphones, lasers, MRI, nuclear power.

must_cover: c is constant for all observers
nothing with mass can reach c
daily_life_hooks: Sunlight takes 8 minutes to reach Earth — you see the Sun as it was 8 minutes ago
When you look at stars, you look into the past — some stars you see may already be dead

must_cover: GPS satellites must correct for this
daily_life_hooks: GPS satellites: their clocks tick 38 microseconds faster per day due to relativity. Without correction, GPS would drift 10 km/day
Astronaut Scott Kelly aged slightly less than his twin on Earth — real, measured, verified
Muons from cosmic rays reach Earth's surface only because time dilation extends their lifetime

must_cover: mass of atom < sum of parts (binding energy)
daily_life_hooks: The Sun converts 4 million tons of mass to energy every SECOND — that's what powers life on Earth
Nuclear power: 1 kg of uranium = energy of 3 million kg of coal
PET scans: antimatter meets matter → pure energy conversion inside your body (tiny amounts!)
Neon signs: specific colors because electrons can only jump between specific energy levels
LED colors: the material determines the energy gap → determines the color
Lasers: all photons same energy = same color = coherent light

must_cover: cannot know position and momentum exactly simultaneously
daily_life_hooks: Electron microscopes have a resolution limit set by uncertainty principle
Quantum tunneling (enabled by uncertainty) makes computer flash memory possible
The Sun wouldn't shine without quantum tunneling — protons tunnel through their repulsion barrier to fuse

10_nuclear_physics_and_cosmology.philosophy: Every atom in your body was forged inside a star. The carbon in your DNA, the iron in your blood, the calcium in your bones — all created by nuclear fusion in stellar cores billions of years ago. Nuclear physics tells the story of where matter comes from and where the universe is going. This is the most epic story physics tells.

daily_life_hooks: The strong force is 100x stronger than electromagnetic force — it takes enormous energy to split an atom
Stable atoms are the ones that survived — unstable ones decayed long ago

must_cover: half-life: time for half to decay
daily_life_hooks: Carbon-14 dating: half-life of 5,730 years → tells us age of ancient artifacts
Smoke detectors use americium-241 (alpha decay)
Bananas are slightly radioactive (potassium-40) — you'd need to eat 10 million to get a dangerous dose
Medical imaging: radioactive tracers with short half-lives to see inside your body
~10% of world electricity comes from nuclear fission
A single uranium fuel pellet (size of a fingertip) = energy of 1 ton of coal
Nuclear submarines can operate for 20+ years without refueling

must_cover: powers the Sun and all stars
produces far less radioactive waste than fission
daily_life_hooks: Every second, the Sun fuses 600 million tons of hydrogen into helium
Fusion is the ultimate clean energy — fuel is hydrogen (from water), waste is helium (party balloons)
Every element heavier than iron was created in supernova explosions — you are literally made of stardust

must_cover: evidence: cosmic microwave background, redshift, light element abundance
daily_life_hooks: The static on an old TV between channels? ~1% of that is actual cosmic microwave background — radiation left over from the Big Bang
Every hydrogen atom in your body has existed since 3 minutes after the Big Bang

must_cover: nebula → main sequence → red giant → white dwarf/neutron star/black hole
heavy elements forged in cores and supernovae
daily_life_hooks: The iron in your blood was forged in the core of a massive star that exploded billions of years ago
The calcium in your bones, the oxygen you breathe, the silicon in your phone — all from dead stars
Carl Sagan: 'We are a way for the cosmos to know itself.' Physics makes that literally true.
